title: Vitamin D — Health Professional Fact Sheet
stance_summary: Situational supplementation recommended for infants (400 IU), adults 75+, pregnant women, and those with limited sun exposure or pre-diabetes. General adult population 19-74 without risk factors should follow IOM RDA (600-800 IU). 2,000 IU tested in VITAL showed no significant CVD reduction but a promising signal for reduced cancer mortality after latency adjustment.

title: Vitamin B12 — Health Professional Fact Sheet
stance_summary: Strongly recommended for vegans/vegetarians (only natural sources are animal foods) and adults 50+ who may malabsorb food-bound B12. Crystalline B12 in supplements and fortified foods is readily absorbed. No UL established. High-dose oral B12 (1,000-2,000 mcg) as effective as IM injections for deficiency per Cochrane Review.

title: Folate — Health Professional Fact Sheet
stance_summary: All women and adolescents capable of pregnancy should consume 400 mcg folic acid daily from supplements and/or fortified foods. During pregnancy: 600 mcg DFE/day. USPSTF reaffirmed A-grade recommendation. CDC advises 400 mcg/day folic acid specifically. UL is 1,000 mcg synthetic folic acid/day.

title: Iron — Health Professional Fact Sheet
stance_summary: Iron supplementation routinely recommended in pregnancy (Cochrane Review: 70% reduced anemia risk, 57% reduced iron deficiency). AAP recommends 1 mg/kg/day for exclusively breastfed infants from 4 months. ACOG recommends low-dose iron starting first trimester. Vegetarians need 1.8x more iron due to reduced nonheme bioavailability.

title: Magnesium — Health Professional Fact Sheet
stance_summary: 48% of Americans consume less than the EAR for magnesium. Supplementation shows moderate evidence for migraine prevention (300 mg twice daily), modest blood pressure reduction (2-4 mmHg). Supplement UL is 350 mg/day for adults (does not apply to food sources). Citrate, malate, and glycinate forms have better bioavailability than oxide.

title: Calcium — Health Professional Fact Sheet
stance_summary: Mixed evidence for supplementation: meta-analysis of 8 RCTs showed 15% fracture and 30% hip fracture risk reduction with 500-1,200 mg/day calcium + vitamin D. However, meta-analysis of 14 RCTs found 15% increased CVD risk and 16% increased CHD risk with calcium supplements. Expert panel found moderate-quality evidence that dietary calcium does not increase CVD risk. USPSTF recommends against lower-dose supplementation in postmenopausal women.

title: Zinc — Health Professional Fact Sheet
stance_summary: Zinc lozenges (>75 mg/day) may reduce cold duration by approximately 2 days if started within 24 hours (2024 Cochrane Review). Vegetarians and vegans have lower zinc bioavailability and may need to supplement. Adult UL is 40 mg/day — excess zinc impairs copper absorption and lowers HDL. Intranasal zinc specifically warned against due to anosmia risk.

title: Vitamin C — Health Professional Fact Sheet
stance_summary: Most nonsmoking adults meet the RDA from diet. Smokers need an extra 35 mg/day due to increased oxidative stress. Supplementation does not reduce cold incidence in the general population, but modestly reduces duration (8% in adults). Regular high-dose vitamin C does not reduce cancer or CVD risk in clinical trials. High-dose vitamin C may carry excess CVD mortality risk in some postmenopausal women with diabetes.

title: Omega-3 Fatty Acids — Health Professional Fact Sheet
stance_summary: Omega-3 supplements lower triglycerides and may reduce CVD risk, especially for those with low fish intake or existing coronary heart disease. AHA recommends ~1 g/day EPA+DHA for existing CHD and 4 g/day prescription omega-3s for hypertriglyceridemia. Evidence for brain health and cognitive function in healthy older adults is not strong in RCTs. Food-first approach (fatty fish 2x/week) preferred.

title: Vitamin E — Health Professional Fact Sheet
stance_summary: Clinical trials show no benefit of vitamin E supplements for CVD or cancer prevention. SELECT trial showed 17% increased prostate cancer risk with 400 IU/day vitamin E. Meta-analyses linked supplementation to small but significant increases in all-cause mortality. High-dose vitamin E increases hemorrhagic stroke risk and can antagonize vitamin K clotting factors. UL is 1,000 mg/day alpha-tocopherol for adults.

title: Vitamin A and Carotenoids — Health Professional Fact Sheet
stance_summary: CARET trial: beta-carotene supplements (30 mg/day) increased lung cancer risk by 28% and lung cancer deaths by 46% in smokers and asbestos-exposed workers. ATBC trial: beta-carotene increased lung cancer by 18% in male smokers. FNB advises against beta-carotene supplements for general population. Pregnant women advised not to exceed 3,000 mcg RAE (10,000 IU) preformed vitamin A due to teratogenic risk. UL for adults: 3,000 mcg RAE preformed vitamin A.

title: Probiotics — Health Professional Fact Sheet
stance_summary: No formal recommendations for or against probiotics in healthy people. LGG and Saccharomyces boulardii have best evidence for preventing antibiotic-associated diarrhea (AAD) — reducing risk by 51%. Specific Lactobacillus/Bifidobacterium combos reduce NEC risk in preterm infants (AGA recommends). IBS: probiotics may reduce symptom persistence by 21%. FDA warns against probiotic use in preterm infants due to infection risk.

title: Selenium — Health Professional Fact Sheet
stance_summary: SELECT trial (35,533 men): selenium 200 mcg/day did not reduce prostate cancer risk overall, and increased high-grade prostate cancer risk in men with already-adequate selenium status. Narrow therapeutic window — UL is 400 mcg/day. Early signs of excess: garlic breath, metallic taste, hair/nail loss. Brazil nuts are an uncontrolled high-dose source. Not recommended for routine supplementation in well-nourished populations.

title: Iodine — Health Professional Fact Sheet
stance_summary: American Thyroid Association and AAP recommend 150 mcg/day iodine supplement for women planning pregnancy, pregnant, or lactating. Only 34 of 59 best-selling prenatal vitamins on market in 2016-17 contained iodine. Only 17.8% of pregnant women took an iodine-containing supplement per 2011-14 NHANES. Iodine is critical for thyroid hormone synthesis and fetal neurological development.

title: Dietary Supplements — FDA Information for Consumers
stance_summary: Under DSHEA (1994), the FDA is not authorized to approve dietary supplements for safety and efficacy before marketing. Manufacturers must ensure products are safe and accurately labeled, but FDA's primary role is reactive. Provides consumer guidance on how to use supplements safely and avoid potentially dangerous products.

title: Vitamin and Mineral Supplements — Nutrition.gov
stance_summary: Government portal aggregating evidence-based dietary supplement information from NIH ODS and USDA. Emphasizes food-first nutrition approaches and provides links to authoritative fact sheets for individual nutrients.

title: A Pharmacist's Guide to Quality Supplements
stance_summary: The most important considerations when pharmacists recommend supplements are product quality, ingredient list, and quality assurance marks. USP Verified Mark is cited as the 'trusted gold standard' and the best way to ensure label accuracy and contaminant safety. Pharmacists should actively guide patients toward third-party verified products.

title: How to Educate Patients About Safety, Regulation, and Quality of Vitamins and Supplements
stance_summary: DSHEA means manufacturers don't need FDA premarket approval. Third-party testing (USP, NSF) is essential for verifying supplement quality. Pradhan-Nabzdyk's goal is to bring 'truth, transparency, and trust' to the supplement industry. Pharmacists should recommend third-party verified products and prioritize food-first nutrition. 'Natural' does not mean safe.

title: Supplements: A Scorecard
stance_summary: Largely skeptical review of popular supplements. Most adults stand to benefit from vitamin D; some from fish oil; and many from fiber. Antioxidant supplements (E, A, beta-carotene) should not be taken. No evidence multivitamins enhance health in well-nourished adults. Selenium not recommended. Calls supplement regulation the 'Wild West' of American health.

title: The World's Leading Vitamin D Experts
stance_summary: Panel of leading vitamin D scientists recommends maintaining serum 25(OH)D at 40-60 ng/mL (100-150 nmol/L). States that 9,600 IU/day from all sources is needed to bring 97.5% of people to 40 ng/mL. Advocates for widespread testing and personalized supplementation, contrasting with IOM/Endocrine Society's more conservative population-level recommendations.

title: What to Do About Your Vitamin D Level
stance_summary: Recommends a 'Test, Adjust, Retest' approach to vitamin D supplementation. Target level: 40-60 ng/mL for general health; some evidence for benefits above 60 ng/mL for autoimmune diseases and cancer. Emphasizes that supplementation dose cannot be assumed without measuring actual serum levels.

title: Vitamin D and Omega-3 Trial (VITAL) — Brigham and Women's Hospital
stance_summary: VITAL: randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled trial in 25,875 US men and women testing 2,000 IU/day vitamin D3 and 1 g/day omega-3 (as Omacor) for primary prevention of CVD and cancer. 4-arm design allows independent assessment of each supplement. Primary investigator JoAnn Manson: 'Vitamin D and omega-3 fatty acids look promising in observational studies but there's a great need for large-scale randomized trials.'

title: VITAL Researcher JoAnn Manson Outlines Findings on Vitamin D and Omega-3
stance_summary: VITAL results: omega-3s showed 28% reduction in heart attack; vitamin D showed 25% reduction in cancer mortality after latency adjustment. For omega-3s, those with low fish intake (<1.5 servings/week) had 19% reduction in primary CVD endpoint and 40% reduction in heart attack. Manson advises against mega-dosing and cautions that VITAL's demonstrated safety applies only to tested doses (2,000 IU vitamin D, 1 g omega-3).

title: Michael Holick — BU School of Medicine Faculty Profile
stance_summary: Holick has made fundamental discoveries in vitamin D biochemistry including identifying 25(OH)D as the circulating form used to measure status. Advocates that up to 10,000 IU/day does not cause toxicity over 5 months and promotes raising awareness of the vitamin D deficiency epidemic, including links to cancer, cardiovascular disease, diabetes, and autoimmune diseases.

title: What Supplements Should I Take? Use Dr. Hyman's Handy Guide
stance_summary: Recommends five foundational supplements for almost everyone: (1) high-quality multivitamin, (2) purified fish oil (EPA/DHA for inflammation, heart, brain), (3) vitamin D3 (5,000 IU/day — up to 95% of Americans deficient), (4) magnesium glycinate (300 mg/day for 600+ enzymatic reactions), (5) prebiotic + probiotic (for gut microbiome integrity). Functional medicine 'fill the gaps' approach.

title: Vitamins: Benefit of the Doubt vs. Doubts About Benefit — Harvard Health
stance_summary: Walter Willett recommends taking a daily multivitamin as a form of nutritional insurance and precaution against poor dietary quality. Argues that RCTs may underestimate supplements' benefits due to insufficient study duration. Notes the notable exceptions: vitamin D and folic acid for women of childbearing age. Diverges from the majority view that evidence is insufficient for routine MVM use.

title: Dr. Rhonda Patrick: Micronutrients for Health and Longevity
stance_summary: Rhonda Patrick shares detailed supplementation protocols: omega-3 at 2-4 g/day EPA+DHA targeting an omega-3 index of 8%; vitamin D3 at 5,000 IU/day maintaining ~50 ng/mL; magnesium in forms matched to need (threonate for cognition, bisglycinate for sleep, citrate for GI). Both educators take a personalized, lab-guided, broadly supportive stance on these supplements.

title: Vitamin D, Calcium, or Combined Supplementation for Primary Prevention of Fractures — USPSTF Final Recommendation
stance_summary: Grade D recommendation against daily supplementation with ≤400 IU vitamin D + ≤1,000 mg calcium in postmenopausal community-dwelling women for primary fracture prevention. Insufficient evidence (Grade I) for higher doses or for men/premenopausal women. Supplementation increases kidney stone risk (small magnitude). Note: a 2024 draft update extends the D-grade recommendation against both fracture and fall prevention across broader populations.

title: Folic Acid Supplementation to Prevent Neural Tube Defects — USPSTF Final Recommendation
stance_summary: Grade A recommendation: all persons planning to or capable of becoming pregnant should take a daily supplement containing 0.4-0.8 mg (400-800 mcg) folic acid. Convincing evidence that periconceptional folic acid substantially reduces neural tube defect risk. Reaffirmation of 2017 recommendation; no new evidence changed the grade.

title: Vitamin D, Iron, and Zinc Supplementation — AAP Policy
stance_summary: AAP recommends 400 IU vitamin D daily for all breastfed infants starting soon after birth. 1 mg/kg/day iron supplement for exclusively or primarily breastfed full-term infants from age 4 months until iron-rich complementary foods are introduced. Policy is universal for breastfed infants because breast milk is low in vitamin D and may not meet iron needs.

title: Healthy Eating During Pregnancy — ACOG FAQ
publisher: American College of Obstetricians and Gynecologists
stance_summary: All pregnant women should take a prenatal vitamin with at least 400 mcg folic acid starting 1 month before pregnancy. Pregnant women need 27 mg iron/day (vs. 18 mg normally) covered by most prenatal vitamins. Calcium 1,000-1,300 mg/day essential for fetal bone development. Vitamin D supplementation recommended if deficiency detected. Emphasizes whole foods first with supplements filling gaps.

title: Omega-3 Polyunsaturated Fatty Acid Supplementation and Cardiovascular Disease — AHA Scientific Advisory
stance_summary: Continues to suggest omega-3 PUFA supplementation (1 g/day) is reasonable for secondary prevention of CHD in patients with recent CHD event such as MI. For hypertriglyceridemia, 4 g/day prescription omega-3s are effective alone or as adjuncts to lipid-lowering medication. Evidence base for primary prevention in general population insufficient to recommend routine use.

title: Vitamin D for the Prevention of Disease — Endocrine Society 2024 Clinical Practice Guideline
stance_summary: 2024 guideline replaces 2011 version. Recommends against empiric supplementation beyond IOM RDAs for healthy adults under 50 (Grade 2, low certainty) and against routine supplementation for adults 50-74 (Grade 2, moderate certainty). Recommends empiric supplementation for: children 1-18 (NTD prevention, RTI), adults 75+ (mortality reduction), pregnant women (preeclampsia/mortality), high-risk pre-diabetics (diabetes prevention). Advises against routine 25(OH)D testing.

title: Calcium Supplementation During Pregnancy for Prevention of Pre-Eclampsia — WHO Recommendation
stance_summary: In populations with low dietary calcium intake, WHO recommends 1.5-2.0 g oral elemental calcium daily for pregnant women to reduce risk of pre-eclampsia. Dose should be split into three doses taken at mealtimes. Iron and calcium supplements should be taken separately due to negative interactions. Context-specific recommendation with moderate-certainty evidence.

title: Folic Acid: Facts for Clinicians — CDC
stance_summary: Folic acid is the only form of folate shown to prevent neural tube defects. All women capable of becoming pregnant should get 400 mcg folic acid daily. US mandatory fortification since 1998 has averted approximately 1,300 NTD-affected births annually. Folic acid is recommended specifically (not MTHF) even for women with MTHFR polymorphisms.

title: Dietary Supplement and Vitamin Certification — NSF International
stance_summary: NSF certifies supplements to NSF/ANSI 173, the only American National Standard for dietary supplement ingredients. Three certification components: label claim review, toxicology review, and contaminant testing. NSF is the only independent organization conducting product testing in its own accredited labs. Annual audits and periodic retesting for ongoing compliance. NSF Certified for Sport extends to banned substance testing for athletes.

title: Vitamin D — Mayo Clinic Patient Guide
stance_summary: Recommends RDA of 600 IU/day (ages 1-70) and 800 IU/day (70+). 1,000-2,000 IU/day is generally safe and achieves adequate blood levels with possible additional benefits. Taking >4,000 IU/day may cause toxicity: GI upset, muscle weakness, heart rhythm issues, kidney stones. At-risk groups for deficiency: older adults, breastfed babies, people with dark skin, obesity, or fat malabsorption conditions.

title: Why Omega-3s Are Good for You — Cleveland Clinic Health Essentials
stance_summary: Omega-3s (EPA and DHA) reduce plaque buildup, lower triglycerides, and may slightly raise HDL. Most adults should eat at least two servings of fish per week. If you have heart disease and don't eat fish, a healthcare professional may recommend EPA and DHA supplements. Food-first approach is preferred; supplements should be discussed with a provider. ALA from plant sources has limited conversion to EPA/DHA.

title: Magnesium Supplement — Examine.com Research Summary
stance_summary: Magnesium supplementation has strong evidence for blood pressure reduction, migraine prophylaxis, and improving blood glucose control. Moderate evidence for improving sleep quality (especially in deficient individuals) and reducing anxiety. Form matters: glycinate and malate for general use; L-threonate may have better brain penetration. Deficiency is common due to poor dietary intake of leafy greens and nuts.

title: Vitamin D Supplement — Examine.com Research Summary
stance_summary: Strong evidence for vitamin D supplementation in deficient individuals for bone health and immune function. Moderate evidence for cancer mortality reduction and diabetes prevention. RCT evidence does not strongly support benefits for CVD in the general population. D3 is more effective than D2 at raising serum 25(OH)D. Deficiency is common, especially in northern latitudes, darker skin tones, and those with limited sun exposure.

title: Start Vetting Your Supplements — Harvard Health
stance_summary: Advises consumers to be skeptical of supplement claims and seek supplements with third-party certification seals. Points to DoD's Operation Supplement Safety (OPSS) Scorecard. Recommends PubMed and NIH ODS as reliable sources for evaluating supplement evidence. Key red flags: extravagant claims, celebrity endorsements, 'more is better' logic, and meaningless terms like 'all-natural' or 'anti-aging'.

title: Toolkit for Sleep — Huberman Lab Newsletter
stance_summary: Huberman recommends 145 mg Magnesium L-Threonate or 200 mg Magnesium Bisglycinate 30-60 min before bed for sleep quality. Also recommends apigenin (50 mg) and theanine (100-400 mg). Notes that ~5% experience GI upset from magnesium. Advises starting with one supplement at a time and emphasizes that some people do not need any supplements.

title: Multivitamins: Should You Buy This Insurance? — Harvard Health
stance_summary: Walter Willett explicitly recommends taking a daily multivitamin as 'a form of nutritional insurance'. Federal Dietary Guidelines suggest adults over 50 take multivitamins for vitamin B12 absorption. CDC advises all women of childbearing age to take folic acid — a multivitamin is a convenient delivery method. Notes that RCTs may underestimate long-term benefits of vitamins due to short study duration.

title: Fish Oil and Cardiovascular Disease — AHA Scientific Advisory Update
stance_summary: Omega-3 fatty acids lower triglyceride levels and increase HDL levels. They may also decrease platelet aggregation. Summary of evidence for use in specific CVD risk populations and secondary prevention.

title: Vitamin D: Screening and Supplementation During Pregnancy — ACOG Committee Opinion
publisher: American College of Obstetricians and Gynecologists
stance_summary: When vitamin D deficiency is identified during pregnancy, most experts agree 1,000-2,000 IU/day is safe. Routine screening for all pregnant women not currently recommended due to insufficient evidence. Most prenatal vitamins contain 400 IU vitamin D — which may be inadequate for deficient women. Higher dose regimens for treatment of deficiency have not been adequately studied during pregnancy.

title: Rhonda Patrick's Supplement Routine — FoundMyFitness
stance_summary: Rhonda Patrick shares her current supplement routine including omega-3 dosing strategy, timing, and why she adjusted her omega-3 dose; her rationale for melatonin supplementation; and endurance-supporting supplements. Emphasizes personalization, regular lab testing, and selecting science-backed brands for supplement quality.

title: Endocrine Society Recommends Healthy Adults Under 75 Not Take Vitamin D Beyond IOM RDA
stance_summary: New Endocrine Society guideline: healthy adults under 75 should not take vitamin D beyond IOM-recommended daily intakes (600-800 IU). Routine 25(OH)D level testing not recommended, including for dark-complexion individuals or those with obesity. Key supported groups: children 1-18, pregnant women, adults 75+, and adults with high-risk prediabetes. Daily, lower-dose vitamin D preferred over non-daily high doses for indicated populations.

title: Vitamin, Mineral, and Multivitamin Supplementation to Prevent CVD and Cancer — USPSTF Final Recommendation
stance_summary: Grade D recommendation against use of beta-carotene or vitamin E supplements for prevention of CVD or cancer. Grade I (insufficient evidence) for multivitamins and other single/paired nutrient supplements (except beta-carotene and vitamin E) for CVD or cancer prevention. Does not apply to pregnant women or those who may become pregnant (folic acid covered separately). Clinicians should use judgment for individual patients.

title: Vitamin K — Health Professional Fact Sheet
stance_summary: Vitamin K is essential for blood clotting (K1) and bone metabolism (K2). No UL established for vitamin K due to low toxicity. Vitamin K from food and supplements can interfere with anticoagulant medications like warfarin — patients on warfarin should maintain consistent vitamin K intake. Adequate intake for adults: 90-120 mcg/day. Most people get sufficient vitamin K from leafy green vegetables.

title: Major Study Finds Omega-3 Supplements May Reduce Risk of Heart Attack — Harvard Gazette
stance_summary: Harvard meta-analysis of 13 RCTs (120,000+ adults) found daily omega-3 supplementation associated with reduced risk of most CVD outcomes including 8% reduced risk of heart attack and CHD death. Associations appeared dose-response: higher omega-3 doses provide greater risk reduction. Manson: food-first, but omega-3 supplementation may have a role in appropriate patients. Published in JAHA.
